goal_11.config.title: Building Productive Cities for an Urbanizing World
goal_11.config.abstract: Cities are the engine of global economic growth and offer greater economic opportunities, diversity in jobs, and more extensive infrastructure relative to rural areas. Yet, the benefits of urbanization are not automatic. As urban populations and cities continue to expand, strategic investment in urban planning will be essential to ensure cities deliver opportunity for all.
goal_11.config.homeFactoidText: Urban residents live in slums globally
goal_11.config.homeTagline: Cities are the engine of global economic growth, but strategic investment in urban planning is essential to avoid slums and inequality.
goal_11.content.#1.text: In just over six decades, the world has become predominantly urban. In 1960, just one billion people lived in urban areas. Today, that number has surged to 4.7 billion—about 58 percent of the world’s population.[reference: wup]
goal_11.content.#2.title: The world is urbanizing, but growth is concentrated in some regions
goal_11.content.#2.subtitle: Urban and rural population by region (millions)
goal_11.content.#2.source: World Bank staff estimates based on the United Nations Population Division's World Urbanization Prospects, National definitions. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SP.RUR.TOTL, SP.URB.TOTL).
goal_11.content.#2.keyFactLabel: Of the world population lives in urban areas today.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#0.text: But this transformation has not unfolded evenly across regions.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#1.text: Over the past 25 years, [emphasis: Europe] [emphasis: and Central Asia] has seen little overall population growth, with some growth in urban populations and a modest decline in rural populations.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#2.text: In [emphasis: North America] and[emphasis:  Latin America & the Caribbean], rural populations have plateaued while cities have continued to expand steadily.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#3.text: In [emphasis: East Asia & the Pacific], urbanization has been particularly dramatic: urban populations have grown rapidly as rural populations declined, with urban population overtaking rural in 2008. This shift was driven not only by migration or population growth but also by the reclassification of rural areas and the expansion of city boundaries, absorbing once-rural areas into urban zones.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#4.text: Meanwhile, in [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], [emphasis: the Middle East and North Africa, Afghanistan] [emphasis: and Pakistan], and [emphasis: South Asia], both rural and urban populations have grown. In Sub-Saharan Africa and in the Middle East and North Africa region, urban populations have expanded at a much faster pace than rural ones.
goal_11.content.#3.text: Urbanization is far from over. By 2050, South Asia is projected to be the only region where most people will still live in rural areas.[reference: wup] But the future of urban growth will look different from the past.
goal_11.content.#4.text: The next chapter of urbanization will not be defined by ever-larger megacities, but by the rapid spread of smaller cities across the globe.[reference: wup]
goal_11.content.#5.text: There is no single definition of a city. A “city” is an urban settlement often defined by each country’s national statistical authorities, typically based on criteria such as population size, population density, administrative status, and the share of non-agricultural activities. A simpler definition by World Urbanization Prospects uses only population density and total population as the key criteria to identify a city. Let’s take a look at how cities have and will evolve as per this definition by 2050.
goal_11.content.#6.title: The number of cities worldwide is projected to nearly double between 2000 and 2050
goal_11.content.#6.subtitle: Number of cities by population size
goal_11.content.#6.source: United Nations, Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division (2025). World Urbanization Prospects.
goal_11.content.#6.scenes.#0.text: In 2000, there were just over 9000 cities in the world.
goal_11.content.#6.scenes.#1.text: By 2025, the world had over 12,000 cities. Small and very small cities (between 250,000 and 1 million residents) drove some of this growth, expanding from roughly 8,700 to 11,600. Meanwhile, megacities — those exceeding 10 million people — grew significantly as well, increasing from 20 in 2000 to 33 in 2025.
goal_11.content.#6.scenes.#2.text: However, in the next 25 years, the number of the world's largest cities is not expected to increase at the same pace. By 2050, projections suggest that there will be only 4 additional megacities — bringing the total to 37.
goal_11.content.#6.scenes.#3.text: Instead, urban growth will be driven by an increase in the number of small and very small cities. By 2050, their number is projected to approach 15,000.
goal_11.content.#8.text: Far from being just a demographic shift, urbanization can be an economic opportunity. Across history, countries have grown richer as they have grown more urban.[reference: econgrowth] Why? Because cities create the conditions that make productivity and innovation thrive.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#9.text: That is why urban expansion often happens along with economic growth.
goal_11.content.#10.title: Urbanization is associated with economic growth
goal_11.content.#10.subtitle: Countries and economies with higher urbanization rates also have higher GDP per capita.
goal_11.content.#10.source: World Bank staff estimates based on the United Nations Population Division's World Urbanization Prospects, National definitions. Country official statistics, National Statistical Organizations and/or Central Banks; National Accounts data files, Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SP.URB.TOTL, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD).
goal_11.content.#11.text: Urbanization enables productivity because it benefits both industries and people. As populations concentrate in cities, the forces that drive productivity, that is, scale, specialization, and innovation tend to strengthen.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#12.text: When firms cluster together, they can function more efficiently. Being close to suppliers, customers, and competitors lowers costs. Large, dense markets make it easier to find specialized workers and match their functions with skills. Proximity to other firms also fosters knowledge spillovers: ideas spread faster, which boosts innovation and growth. Urban hubs are key nodes in trade and transport networks, reducing shipping and coordination costs for goods and services. Better connectivity makes it easier for firms in cities to access global markets and value chains, which can further accelerate growth.[reference: quigley2008] This economic benefit from concentration of people and economic activities is called agglomeration effects.[reference: jedwab2017]
goal_11.content.#13.text: People benefit too. Cities offer a chance at economic mobility, more diversity in occupations, better pay, and better infrastructure.
goal_11.content.#15.text: Jobs across rural and urban areas span a broad spectrum of skill levels. Low-skill roles such as cleaners, farm laborers, and construction workers typically require no specialized training or formal education. Medium-skill roles, including clerical workers, specialized agricultural workers, and tradespeople, demand a moderate level of education alongside practical, job-specific skills. High-skill roles such as professionals, technicians, and managers require advanced education and expertise.[reference: skill-ilo]
goal_11.content.#16.text: Let’s compare the “specialization of each skill” in urban and rural areas.
goal_11.content.#17.title: Urban areas offer jobs of a variety of skills
goal_11.content.#17.subtitle: Specialization of jobs by skill level across rural and urban areas. If a particular skill-level job is more specialized in urban areas than employment overall, the indicator is above 1 for that country. When the value is closer to one, the jobs in this skill-level are distributed much like other jobs. Values below one suggest that jobs in this skill level are relatively less specialized in cities.
goal_11.content.#17.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). ILOSTAT Rural and Urban Labour Markets (RURBAN), EMP_TEMP_SEX_OCU_GEO_NB_A.
goal_11.content.#17.note: The specialization indicator is calculated for each country by skill level and location (urban rural). The specialization for high skill in urban areas is calculated by first calculating high skill jobs in urban areas as a share of high skill jobs across all locations, then by calculating urban jobs across all jobs. Finally, we take a ratio of the share of high skill jobs across all locations by urban jobs across all jobs to calculate the specialization. Specialization is calculated in a similar way for each skill and location.
goal_11.content.#17.scenes.#0.text: Each [emphasis: dot represents a country]. If a job is more specialized than employment overall, the indicator is above 1 for that country. When the value is closer to one, the job is distributed much like other jobs. Values below one suggest that jobs are relatively less specialized. This is reported for urban and rural areas and for three skill levels in each country.
goal_11.content.#17.scenes.#1.text: Let’s take [emphasis: Indonesia] for example, where the specialization value for low skill jobs in rural areas is 1.07 and in urban areas is 0.95. This means low skill jobs, compared to all jobs, are 7 percent more common in rural areas and 5 percent less common in urban areas.
goal_11.content.#17.scenes.#2.text: Similarly, medium skill jobs are only 5 percent more common in rural areas and 5 percent less common in urban areas.
goal_11.content.#17.scenes.#3.text: High skill jobs have the starkest contrast: they are 30 percent more common in urban areas and 40 percent less common in rural areas.
goal_11.content.#17.scenes.#4.text: More generally, low- and medium-skilled jobs are specialized in rural areas and roughly in proportion to overall employment in urban areas. By contrast, high-skill jobs are strongly represented in urban areas, with specialization rates far higher in urban areas than in rural areas highlighting cities’ role as hubs for high-skill work.
goal_11.content.#18.text: In many low- and middle-income countries or economies, even low-wage jobs (which also tend to be informal) in cities can offer pathways to build skills, gain experience, and move into better opportunities over time.[reference: wb2021]
goal_11.content.#19.text: Prosperity depends on how well countries attract productive workers, match them to the right jobs, and help them continue to build skills. Cities make this easier because skilled workers learn faster when they are surrounded by peers who have similar or complementary skills, ideas, and experience. The result is higher productivity in urban areas, reinforcing cities as engines of economic growth.[reference: wdr2013]
goal_11.content.#21.text: This diversity in skill levels also means a wider range of occupations.
goal_11.content.#22.text: Urban areas widen the range of jobs people can do. By concentrating commerce, industry, transport, and services in one place, cities create far more varied labor demand than rural areas, where work is largely centered on agriculture.[reference: quigley2008] Cities can support both everyday occupations and highly specialized roles that rural economies cannot sustain. Larger cities, in particular, don’t just offer a greater number of similar jobs, they reorganize work into more specialized and interconnected roles.[reference: task2002]
goal_11.content.#23.title: Urban areas offer a variety of occupations
goal_11.content.#23.subtitle: Specialization of jobs by occupation across rural and urban areas
goal_11.content.#23.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). ILOSTAT Rural and Urban Labour Markets (RURBAN), EMP_TEMP_SEX_OCU_GEO_NB_A.
goal_11.content.#23.note: The specialization indicator is calculated for each country by occupation and location (urban rural). The specialization for Managers in urban areas is calculated by first calculating Manager jobs in urban areas as a share of Manager jobs across all locations, then by calculating urban jobs across all jobs. Finally, we take a ratio of the share of Manager jobs across all locations by urban jobs across all jobs to calculate the specialization. Specialization is calculated in a similar way for each occupation and location.
goal_11.content.#23.textParagraph: Across almost all occupations—except agriculture, forestry, and fishing—jobs are more specialized in urban areas. This highlights how cities offer a far wider and more diverse set of work opportunities for workers than rural areas.[reference: task2002]
goal_11.content.#23.label_OCU_ISCO08_8: Plant and machine operators, and assemblers
goal_11.content.#24.text: For many workers, urban labor markets also mean mobility. In cities, people can easily move between employers and sectors, and sometimes even between informal self-employment and wage work. The density of opportunities in cities makes it easier to keep searching for a better match between skills, preferences, and pay.[reference: blog-jobs]
goal_11.content.#25.text: This diversity of occupations also acts as a form of insurance. When one industry slows, workers in cities can often shift into other sectors without having to move away.[reference: blog-li] [reference: urban-sustainability]
goal_11.content.#27.text: Jobs in cities tend to be higher skilled, formal, and more productive—and that usually means higher pay.[reference: urban pay] Cities bring together larger and more diverse labor markets, with more firms competing for workers, especially in higher-skill sectors.[reference: thickermarket] This competition, along with better infrastructure and job matching, helps push wages up.[reference: compet-pay]
goal_11.content.#28.title: In most countries, hourly earnings are higher in urban than rural areas
goal_11.content.#28.subtitle: Ratio of hourly earning of urban population over rural population
goal_11.content.#28.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). SDG Labour Market Indicators (ILOSDG), EAR_4HRL_SEX_GEO_CUR_NB_A.
goal_11.content.#28.textParagraph: Of the 79 countries for which we have data, average hourly wages are higher in urban areas than in rural areas in 66 countries (that is over 80 percent). For example, in Benin and Peru the average hourly wages in urban areas are 40 percent higher than rural areas.[reference: paydata]
goal_11.content.#29.text: Of course, city life is also more expensive. Rents are higher, and congestion and crowding come with real costs—which means that higher urban wages often do little more than offset the added expenses of city living.[reference: offset-pay] But for many workers and firms, the benefits still outweigh the downsides. Higher lifetime earnings, better job options, and more opportunities to learn and grow make urban areas attractive.[reference: city-better]
goal_11.content.#30.text: But these benefits are not automatic; they depend on investment and planning.
goal_11.content.#32.text: What does urbanization actually look like on the ground? Take Addis Ababa, the capital of Ethiopia. In 1975, fewer than a million people lived there. Over the next 50 years, the city grew dramatically — by 2025, its population had reached over 6.7 million.[reference: wup-degree-urbanization]
goal_11.content.#33.text: Its physical footprint grew just as fast. This can be measured by total built-up area: the total land surface covered by human-made structures and surfaces, including buildings, roads, and paved land. Unlike administrative boundaries, which may include undeveloped land, built-up area captures the city's true physical extent.[reference: builtuparea]
goal_11.content.#34.title: Built-up area in Addis Ababa has more than tripled in the last five decades
goal_11.content.#34.subtitle: Built-up area (km²) and population of Addis Ababa, 1975-2025
goal_11.content.#34.source: Pesaresi et al. (2024). Advances on the Global Human Settlement Layer by joint assessment of Earth Observation and population survey data. International Journal of Digital Earth, 17(1).
goal_11.content.#34.textParagraph: In 1975, Addis Ababa had nearly 39 km² of built-up area. By 2025, that figure had grown to 128 km².[reference: ghsl]
goal_11.content.#35.text: When such expansion takes place, cities have the potential to boost productivity and incomes—but urbanization doesn’t automatically lead to development. Cities that deliver on their promise are those that have planned and built infrastructure ahead of population growth.[reference: planning]
goal_11.content.#36.text: Without that foresight, rapid urbanization can give rise to slums, overcrowding, and limited economic opportunity. Governments must therefore treat infrastructure planning not as a reaction to urban growth, but as a precondition for it.
goal_11.content.#37.text: Slums: A Sign of Unmet Demand
goal_11.content.#38.text: Formal housing markets tend to serve middle- and higher-income households, leaving poorer residents with few options but to settle informally. The growth of slums reflects this imbalance: demand for land and housing rises faster than governments and markets can provide affordable homes.
goal_11.content.#39.text: Slums suffer from severely inadequate physical conditions, including overcrowding, unsafe housing, lack of clean water and sanitation that create serious health risks. These conditions drive the spread of infectious diseases like tuberculosis, worsen mental health through noise, pollution, and lack of green space, and contribute to domestic injuries, disproportionately affecting children and the elderly.[reference: who-slums]
goal_11.content.#40.title: People living in slum conditions
goal_11.content.#40.label: In 2024, globally[reference: wdi-slum]
goal_11.content.#41.text: Today, nearly 1 in 4 urban residents, that is 23% of the world’s urban population live in slum conditions, with the challenge particularly acute in low-income countries where progress in reducing slum populations has slowed. Although the share of people living in slums has declined globally over the past decades, progress often stalls after an initial improvement. In Latin America and the Caribbean, for example, the slum population share fell steadily in the early 2000s but has hovered around 17–19% in recent years. Similarly, in Sub-Saharan Africa, slum population fell from every 6 out of 10 urban residents living in slums to about 5 out of 10 in 2020. But since 2020, the share of people living in slums is rising again.
goal_11.content.#42.title: Decline in slum population has slowed in recent years
goal_11.content.#42.subtitle: Population living in slums (% of urban population), 2000-2022
goal_11.content.#42.source: Urban Indicators Database, UN Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EN.POP.SLUM.UR.ZS).
goal_11.content.#42.y_title: Population living in slums (% of urban population)
goal_11.content.#43.text: Even where the share of urban populations living in slums has stabilized or declined, the aggregate picture remains deeply concerning. In cities experiencing rapid population growth, a stable or falling slum share still translates into a rising absolute number of people living in inadequate conditions.
goal_11.content.#44.text: Globally, the number of slum dwellers grew from approximately 840 million in 2018 to over one billion by 2022 — an increase of more than 160 million people in just four years.
goal_11.content.#45.title: Globally, number of people living in slums has increased by 25% in just 4 years
goal_11.content.#45.subtitle: Total number of people living in slums
goal_11.content.#45.source: Urban Indicators Database, UN Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EN.POP.SLUM.UR.ZS).
goal_11.content.#45.square_represents: Every square represents 1 million people living in slums
goal_11.content.#46.text: Economic growth can help reduce slums by raising incomes and enabling greater investment in housing and infrastructure, but without careful urban planning and adequate housing supply, slums can persist even in growing economies.[reference: slums]
goal_11.content.#48.text: As cities expand, much more land will need to be planned and serviced. Built-up areas are projected to grow by more than [emphasis: 50 percent over the next 25 years], largely in lower-middle income and upper-middle income countries, making the way cities expand increasingly important.
goal_11.content.#49.title: Built-up area will expand by more than 50 percent over the next 25 years
goal_11.content.#49.source: Pesaresi et al. (2024). Advances on the Global Human Settlement Layer by joint assessment of Earth Observation and population survey data. International Journal of Digital Earth, 17(1).
goal_11.content.#49.keyFactLabel: Increase in urban built-up area is expected in the next 25 years
goal_11.content.#50.text: For cities to deliver on their productive potential, how they grow matters as much as how fast they grow. A fragmented city, where housing, jobs, and services are disconnected, where infrastructure does not extend to where people actually live, and where institutions lack the capacity to manage expansion, undermines the very agglomeration benefits that make urban areas valuable in the first place.
goal_11.content.#51.text: When growth is poorly managed, the result is high living costs, sprawling informal settlements, and social exclusion, with productivity held back rather than amplified. The alternative is not to restrict urban growth, but to shape it. This means planning ahead of expansion rather than reacting to it: extending road networks and services before populations arrive, encouraging efficient densities that make infrastructure investment viable, and ensuring that land use connects people to opportunity. Cities in Sub-Saharan Africa, where urban populations are growing fastest, face this challenge most acutely. At the same time, the pace of expansion means that large parts of the urban environment have yet to be built, giving today’s planning and investment decisions lasting influence over future productivity and opportunity.
goal_11.references.keyOfReference: Here goes the content of the reference, which can include links like this. Add a reference in the text with [reference: keyOfReference]

goal_11.references.wb2021: World Bank — Migrants, Markets, and Mayors: Rising Above the Employment Challenge in Africa's Secondary Cities (2021) (link)

goal_11.references.offset-pay: Edward Glaeser (2014), Housing Dynamics
goal_11.references.city-better: Giuliano, G., Kang, S. & Yuan, Q. Agglomeration economies and evolving urban form. Ann Reg Sci 63, 377–398 (2019). https://doi.org/10.1007/s00168-019-00957-4
goal_11.references.builtuparea: World Bank. (2004). The Spatial Organization of Cities: Deliberate Outcome or Unforeseen Consequence? Washington, DC: World Bank. (link)
goal_11.references.planning: Watanabe, A. (2023). Labor Market and Urban Wage Premium. In: Zimmermann, K.F. (eds) Handbook of Labor, Human Resources and Population Economics. Springer, Cham. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-57365-6_415-1
goal_11.references.slums: World Bank Blog, "Expanding Opportunities for Urban Poor in East Asia and Pacific Cities," Victoria Kwakwa (2018)
